entry.title: How to use the new Google IDE Antigravity with SAP (and CAP)
entry.content:
We hear that with the new SAP ABAP Cloud on Visual Studio Code, more and more developers will move to the “new” IDE.
We already know that Business Application Studio is a cousin of Visual Studio Code.
Do you know who another cousin is?

Spoiler
Google Antigravity!

In this brief blog post, I will present what it is, why you should use it, how to install all components, how to use it with SAP, and my final thoughts after an intensive weekend of use.

This guide focuses on Windows and Node.js installation (no Java). ⚠️
My personal impressions are from an Apple user perspective. 🤙

What is Google Antigravity?

Google Antigravity is a new IDE forked from Visual Studio Code.
I started using it based on a colleague’s suggestion, mainly because:

  • Advanced agent-based development model
  • Google as a strong and reliable engineering brand
  • Support for running multiple agents in parallel
  • Unique planning and “vibe coding” experience
  • Temporary free access to Gemini Pro and Opus models (only for this you should try it!)

Why not give it a try?

Download Antigravity

Google Antigravity Download

If you have never used Visual Studio Code before

The following steps help you get started if you are completely new to Visual Studio Code and Antigravity.

 

1. Install Node.js

Download the latest Node.js version and complete the installation:

Node.js — Download Node.js®

 

2. Install CDS Tools for CAP Development

Install the CAP development kit globally:

npm i -g @sap/cds-dk

Note: the -g parameter installs the tool globally and only needs to be executed once.

3. Install Cloud Foundry CLI

The CF CLI is required for Cloud Foundry operations such as application deployment and service management.

You can download it from the SAP Development Tools page (Cloud tab):

SAP Development Tools

 

Verify the installation:

cf -v

If Antigravity was already open during installation, restart it.

Install the Multi-Target Application plugin:

cf install-plugin multiapps

5. Install Chocolatey and MTA Build Tool (MBT)

Chocolatey is optional. I use it to install GNU Make, which is required by the MTA Build Tool during the build process.

Chocolatey installation guide:

Installing Chocolatey

Install the MTA Build Tool:

npm install -g mbt

Verify the installation:

mbt -v

Configure Plugins and Marketplace for SAP in Antigravity

Antigravity is based on an open-source ecosystem and does not have direct access to all official VS Code plugins. This requires a small configuration change.

From my perspective, the most important plugins are:

  • SAP Fiori Tools
  • CDS Tools
  • CAP Tools
  • SAPUI5 Extension
  • UI5 XML Support
  • Remote Access for SAP Business Application Studio

Go to File > Preferences > Antigravity Settings

 

Set the following values:

 

Marketplace Item URL:
https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items

Marketplace Gallery URL:
https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/_apis/public/gallery

Please restart Antigravity after this change ⚠️

Install all your favorite plugins:

 

Configure MCP Server

At this point, I recommend configuring the MCP server with the Antigravity AI chat. Here I only describe the CAP MCP configuration, but if you want more info, this is the original post with the most important MCP servers: SAP Build introduces new MCP Servers to enable age... - SAP Community.

In the agent view, select the three dots (…) and choose MCP Servers


, then Manage MCP Servers,


and finally View raw configuration.

Paste the following configuration:

{
  "servers": {
    "cds-mcp": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@cap-js/mcp-server"],
      "env": {},
      "type": "stdio"
    }
  },
  "inputs": []
}


Restart Antigravity to complete the setup. ⚠️

To learn more about MCP servers, refer to: MCP – A Comprehensive Guide

Final Conclusion after Intensive Use

Antigravity is still a young product. I had to restart it multiple times during my work, and I consumed the Gemini Pro and Opus credits fairly quickly.

Visual Studio Code still feels more stable, and SAP’s tooling is clearly more mature there. However, switching between the two is extremely easy, which makes experimentation low risk.

I did not fully experience the benefits of multi-model usage yet—this may be due to configuration or limited testing time. Still, I recommend exploring it and trying it out during your vibe coding sessions.

Let me know how your experience goes and what your impression is of this new IDE.
Pro or Cons?

entry.title: Using Cline with SAP AI Core in VS Code: A Complete Setup Guide
entry.content:
As SAP developers, we spend our days juggling multiple tools such as code editors, documentation pages, AI Launchpad, and API testing platforms. With information scattered across different places, a significant amount of time is lost switching between tabs instead of focusing on actual development. Now imagine if SAP’s GenAI capabilities were available right where you work, ready to support you the moment you need them.

That is exactly what Cline integrated with SAP AI Core delivers. Cline works like an AI coding partner inside your development environment, and when connected to SAP AI Core, it brings SAP’s GenAI Hub models directly into your workflow. There are no API calls to write and no dashboards to open. You simply type a prompt and receive instant, SAP-aware assistance for CAP, UI5, CDS, integrations, and more.

Setting it up is simple. Install two extensions and connect your SAP AI Core service key. In just a few minutes, your development environment transforms into an AI-enhanced workspace where you can generate code, debug faster, understand complex logic, and boost productivity without breaking your flow.

This blog walks you through that setup and helps you experience how seamless and powerful SAP development becomes when GenAI is built directly into your everyday tools.

Before you start using Cline with SAP AI Core, please ensure that your SAP GenAI Hub environment is already set up.
If you have not completed the setup, please follow the links below:

  • SAP AI Core provisioning
    https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-core-genaihub-provisioning..html
  • SAP AI Launchpad provisioning
    https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-launchpad-provisioning..html

Step 1: Install Cline Extensions in VS Code

  1. Open Visual Studio Code
  2. Go to the Extensions tab

  1. Search for "Cline"
  2. You will see two extensions in the results

  1. Install both: Cline & Cline for SAP AI Core

After installation, close and reopen VS Code.

Step 2: Create or Use an SAP AI Core Instance

Open your SAP BTP Subaccount.
If you do not already have an SAP AI Core instance, create one by following the provisioning guide: https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-core-genaihub-provisioning..html

Once the instance is created:

 

  1. Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Locate your AI Core instance
  3. Create a Service Key
  4. You will use the details from this service key to configure Cline

Example service key:

Step 3: Configure Cline With SAP AI Core Credentials

In Cline,
API Provider: SAP AI Core
AI Core Client Id: <take the info from key : clientid>
AI Core Client Secret: < take the info from key : clientsecret>
AI Core Base URL: <take the info from key : AI_API_URL>
AI Core Auth URL: <take the info from key : url>
AI Core Resource Group: <by default, group will be default. If you have any specific group please specify the name>
Orchestration Mode: If you have an extended service plan, the “Orchestration Mode” checkbox will automatically appear
       Note: If Orchestration Mode is checked, you instantly get access to all models via the Harmonized API with no deployments, else you must deploy and manage each model separately.
Model:  Choose desired model from the “dropdown”

Step 4: Verify That Cline Is Working

  1. Click the Cline icon in the VS Code sidebar
  2. Type a test prompt, such as: “Write a 1-line JavaScript function.”
  3. Press Enter

You should now see the response.
🎉 Cline is successfully connected to SAP AI Core!



By integrating Cline with SAP AI Core, you now have a practical and efficient way to use SAP’s GenAI capabilities directly within your development workflow. This setup allows you to generate CAP service logic, refine CDS models, create UI5 components, validate integration flows, and troubleshoot code without jumping between multiple tools.

Using your AI Core service key, endpoints, and resource group, Cline securely connects to SAP’s GenAI Hub and gives you quick access to both Harmonized and model-specific APIs. This ensures that your prompts return consistent, SAP-relevant outputs that fit naturally into your existing project structure.

With everything configured and your first prompt tested, you can now explore different models, try more advanced scenarios, and make AI a reliable part of your daily SAP development routine. This simple addition can make your workflow faster, cleaner, and much more enjoyable.

E2E information available in the attached blog - https://docs.cline.bot/provider-config/sap-aicore

entry.title: Accelerating SAPUI5 Migration to BAS: A Practical Framework
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP has transitioned its development paradigm from SAP Web IDE to SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), pushing developers to migrate from legacy UI5 to Fiori applications to the new environment. This paper outlines a structured and scalable methodology to migrate SAP UI5 applications from WEB IDE to BAS, focusing on connectivity, tooling and configuration challenges.

Methodology:

  1. Log in to your BTP Account. Go into your space. Go to the Service Marketplace. Search for Business Application Studio. Open Business Application Studio Application.

 

Note: Pre-Requisite - Cloud Connector and Destination should be set up for connectivity with the S4.

2. Click on Create Dev Space. Give some Dev Space Name—select the SAP Fiori on the left. On the right side, under Additional SAP Extensions select HTML5 Application Template, Launchpad Module, Basic UI5 Template. Click on Create Dev Space.

3. Dev Space is created as shown below. It will take a few minutes to start. Once it's started, click on the Dev space. Here, I have named it “DemoMigration”. It will open the Development workspace. Bookmark this URL for future use and easy navigation to Workspace.

4. For the first time, click on the menu on the top left and select the open folder. Search for Projects Folder. Click on OK.

5. Click on Import on your Get Started page. It will prompt to start the migration. Select migration option. Below screen will appear.  

 

6. Select Destination and Hostname and Client Details.

Conclusion:

Migrating SAP UI5 applications from SAP Web IDE to SAP Business Application Studio is more than a technical upgrade. It represents a strategic change towards a cloud-native and future ready development environment aligned with that of SAP’s roadmap. This blog outlined a well-structured migration methodology, which includes prerequisite connectivity set up, dev space configuration, project import and environment preparation within BAS. By following these steps, organizations can ensure a smooth transition while minimizing disruption to existing Fiori applications. This structured approach simplifies migration from WEB IDE to BAS. It supports scalable and cloud-aligned SAPUI5 development for public institutions and enterprises.

.

entry.title: Part 1: Creating and Deploying a SAP CAP Application with SAP HANA on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry)
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the First Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone

 

Introduction

SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is often demonstrated through local examples, but its real value becomes clear when the application is deployed to SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and backed by a managed database.

In this blog, we will go through creating a basic SAP CAP application using the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) CAP template, binding it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deploying it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime.

The goal here is not to build a complex application, but to establish a clean, working reference point. I start with a minimal CAP project to keep the focus on deployment rather than domain complexity.

This blog serves as a baseline for a series where the same CAP application will later be deployed using different databases and runtimes. The focus here is not application complexity, but establishing a clean, working reference point.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • Active Hana Cloud Instance
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

 

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a CAP Project from Template given in the BAS:

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-hana-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: SAP HANA Cloud
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

 

CAP Project creation wizard with SAP HANA selected

 

This will create a CAP project for you with mta.yaml automatically updated. The generated project follows standard CAP structure:

  • app –  UI
  • db – Schema and data files
  • srv – Service Definition

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Development:

Follow the following steps:

  • Define a Simple Data Model:

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;
entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

               

  • Expose the Model via CAP Service:

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

  • Add Data:

Run the following in the project terminal:

cds add data --records 5

 

  • Run the Application locally:

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build
cds deploy

 

  • Add a Managed Approuter:
  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

 

This is important to view the data once deployed.

 

Prepare the Project for Deployment:

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

 

Build the CAP Project:

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application:

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry deployment

 

During deployment:

  • HDI Container is created
  • Database schema is deployed
  • CAP Service is bound to the database automatically

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application:

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

With that, we have successfully created a minimal SAP CAP application, connected it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime. While the application itself is intentionally simple, the deployment pipeline we established here — from schema definition to MTA build to Cloud Foundry deployment — reflects a production-ready workflow that scales well as complexity grows.

The key takeaways from this exercise are that the BAS CAP template significantly reduces boilerplate by auto-generating the mta.yaml with the necessary modules and bindings, the HDI container handles schema deployment seamlessly, and CAP's convention-over-configuration approach means your OData service is ready without any manual REST wiring.

This baseline will serve as the foundation for the rest of this series, where we will explore deploying the same application with different databases, runtimes, and configurations — making it easier to understand the trade-offs of each approach. Stay tuned for the next part!

 

Next Steps

In the upcoming blogs, we will focus on creating the same application using a PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option.

entry.title: Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean Core, BTP Driven Strategy for ModernUtilities
entry.content:
Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean‑Core, BTP‑Driven Strategy for Modern Utilities

Executive Summary

If you’ve spent any time around utility billing operations, you know mass reversals aren’t rare — they’re part of the job. Sometimes it’s a rate issue, sometimes a meter read problem, and sometimes it’s just one of those unexpected data surprises that ripple across months of billing, these reversals often span multiple billing cycles and thousands of customers. While the operational process may take days to complete, the customer impact is immediate: as soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on their online portals. This triggers confusion, social media chatter, call‑center spikes, and reputational risk before corrected bills are ready.

What I want to share here is a practical way utilities can handle this without rewriting IS‑U or scrambling teams at 6 AM. We used SAP BTP to create a small visibility layer that sits between IS‑U and the customer portal — and honestly, it solved a problem that has bothered billing teams for years, event‑driven architecture, and lightweight AI to prevent premature credit visibility without modifying SAP IS‑U or customer‑facing systems. The solution introduces a “Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer” that controls what customers see during mass reversals, ensuring operational accuracy without customer panic.

The Practitioner Reality: When a Simple Reversal Becomes a Public Crisis

Every utility billing team has lived through this scenario:

  • A mass correction requires reversing 8–12 months of invoices.
  • The reversal job runs for hours or days.
  • As soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on the portal.
  • Screenshots appear on social media: “Utility X just gave me $900 credit!”
  • Call centers are overwhelmed.
  • Regulators ask questions.
  • The billing team is still in the middle of the correction.

The root cause is simple: SAP IS‑U posts reversals immediately, and customer portals read them immediately. And here’s the part nobody talks about: IS‑U has not built‑in buffer. The moment a reversal posts, the customer sees it. There’s no ‘hold this until we’re done’ button. That’s where the trouble starts. Utilities cannot legally notify customers in advance, and they cannot delay the operational correction. This creates a structural gap between internal billing processes and external customer visibility.

Why This Happens: A Technical Gap in Traditional IS‑U

SAP IS‑U was designed with a real‑time posting model:

  • Reversal document posts →
  • FICA updates →
  • Portal reads →
  • Customer sees credit

There is no concept of:

  • “Temporary suppression”
  • “Billing in progress”
  • “Visibility hold”
  • “Batch publish”

This is not a failure of IS‑U — it’s simply a design that predates modern digital customer expectations.

The Modern Solution: A Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP

Instead of modifying IS‑U or customer portals, utilities can introduce a lightweight, clean‑core extension on SAP BTP that controls visibility, not billing.

This layer sits between:

SAP IS‑U → BTP → Customer Portal / CIS / Mobile App

It does not change billing logic. It does not alter FICA. It does not modify the portal. It simply orchestrates when billing information becomes visible.

Key Capabilities

  • Detect mass reversals in real time
  • Flag accounts as “under correction”
  • Temporarily suppress credit visibility
  • Replace credit display with a neutral message
  • Release final billing once corrections are complete

This simple layer kept customers from seeing half‑baked credits and gave the billing team the breathing room they needed to finish the job properly.

Textual Representation

 

How It Works Using BTP Services

1. SAP Event Mesh — Detect Reversals in Real Time

Event Mesh listens for:

  • Billing document reversals
  • FKK document reversals
  • Mass job triggers

When reversal volume exceeds a threshold (e.g., >500 accounts), it triggers the suppression workflow.

2. SAP BTP Workflow — Manage the Correction Window

Workflow orchestrates:

  • Start of reversal window
  • Suppression period
  • Completion of rebilling
  • Release of final invoice

This creates a controlled, auditable process.

3. SAP BTP Business Rules — Define Visibility Logic

Rules can be configured without coding:

  • “If reversal count > X, suppress visibility for Y hours.”
  • “If account is in correction, show neutral message.”
  • “If rebill fails, extend suppression.”

This keeps the solution clean‑core and upgrade‑safe.

4. SAP HANA Cloud — Temporary Data Store

Stores:

  • Accounts under correction
  • Suppression flags
  • Workflow status
  • Rebill completion indicators

No changes to IS‑U tables.

5. SAP BTP AI — Predict and Prevent Customer Impact

AI can:

  • Identify accounts likely to call
  • Detect social media spikes
  • Predict rebill duration
  • Recommend suppression windows
  • Flag anomalies in reversal patterns

This turns a reactive process into a proactive one.

What the Customer Sees Instead

Instead of a large credit, the portal displays:

“Your billing is being updated. Your final balance will be available shortly.”

This avoids:

  • Panic
  • Misinterpretation
  • Social media escalation
  • Call center overload

Clean‑Core Alignment

This approach is fully clean‑core because:

  • No IS‑U modifications
  • No FICA changes
  • No portal rewrites
  • No custom Z‑tables in SAP
  • All logic runs on BTP
  • Fully upgrade‑safe
  • Works with ECC and S/4HANA

This is exactly what SAP means by “extensibility without modification.”

Practitioner Example

In one of the utilities I worked with, we had to reverse nearly 10 months of bills for over 180,000 customers. Within 45 minutes, screenshots of huge credits were all over social media. The call center was drowning. Regulators were pinging leadership. It was chaos — and all because the portal updated too early.

After implementing a BTP‑based visibility layer:

  • Mass reversals ran silently
  • No customer saw temporary credits
  • No social media chatter
  • No call center spike
  • Corrected bills were published cleanly

The billing team completed the same process with zero customer disruption.

Conclusion

Mass invoice reversals will always be part of utility operations. But customer visibility does not need to be immediate or uncontrolled. By introducing a Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP — powered by Event Mesh, Workflow, Business Rules, HANA Cloud, and AI — utilities can protect customer experience, reduce operational risk, and maintain clean‑core principles.

This is modernization where it matters: not in rewriting billing logic, but in orchestrating how and when customers see the results.

entry.title: Part 2: Creating and Deploying a CAP Application with PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the Second Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone.

 

Introduction

In the Part 1 of this series, a CAP application has been created and deployed to SAP BTP using SAP HANA Cloud. In the current blog, I will focus on creating and deploying a CAP application to BTP using the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option provided by SAP. This approach demonstrates a PostgreSQL-first workflow and focuses on the specific configuration required to use PostgreSQL as the persistence layer.

 

Why PostgreSQL on SAP BTP

You might have a question, “If a CAP application can be deployed with SAP HANA Cloud, then why go with an alternative Database?”

While the SAP HANA Cloud is still the best choice for enterprise applications, CAP also allows seamless integration with alternative databases with minimal changes.

PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP is useful in scenarios such as:

  • Cost-sensitive environments
  • Open-source aligned architectures
  • Hybrid cloud landscapes

From the CAP application perspective, the CDS model and service definitions remain unchanged.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option service subcription
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a New CAP Project in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-postgres-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: PostgreSQL
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

CAP project creation wizard

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Define the Data Model

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;

entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

 

Expose the Model via a CAP Service

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

Add Data

Run the following in BAS Terminal

cds add data –records 5

 

Run the Application locally

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build

cds deploy

 

Add a Managed Approuter

  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

This is important to view the data once deployed.

Prepare the Project for Deployment

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

Though everything is ready, you need to make a small change for Postgres Deployment. Make the following change:

  • In MTA, go to resources
  • Navigate to <app-name>-postgres
  • Change the service-plan from development to trial

 

mta.yaml visible in the BAS workspace

 

Note : Do this only if you are using a Trial Account. If you are in an enterprise account, please go to your Instances and Subscription and copy the service-plan from PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option and look for the plan under Service Plan.

 

Configure CAP to Use PostgreSQL

Navigate into the project folder and install the PostgreSQL driver:

cds add postgres

Note: This step is not important as BAS Template must have already added this. This could be used while creating the CAP application without the Template wizard.

 

Build the CAP Project

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-postgres-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry Deployment

 

During deployment

  • PostgreSQL credentials are injected automatically via service binding
  • CAP initializes the database schema
  • The application starts without additional configuration

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

Deploying an SAP CAP application with the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP highlights CAP’s database-agnostic architecture. The CDS models and service logic remain unchanged, while PostgreSQL is integrated seamlessly through minimal configuration and service binding.

This approach provides flexibility for cost-sensitive and open-source aligned environments while still leveraging SAP BTP’s enterprise capabilities such as automated schema deployment, secure credential management, and simplified lifecycle handling through Cloud Foundry and MTA deployment.

Overall, PostgreSQL on SAP BTP enables developers to build scalable, cloud-native CAP applications with greater persistence flexibility, while retaining the option to transition to SAP HANA Cloud in the future without significant code changes.

 

Next Steps

In the next part of this series, the deployed CAP application will be integrated with SAP Build Work Zone, demonstrating how backend services can be exposed through a secure, user-friendly interface to deliver a complete end-to-end enterprise application experience.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 4 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  HANA CLOUD  |  RAG

SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store

End-to-end RAG pipeline on SAP data — REAL_VECTOR schema setup, SAP embedding model, document chunking, similarity search, and four working use cases: knowledge base search, data dictionary retrieval, transport log search, and master data context retrieval.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 4 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: HANA Cloud's native REAL_VECTOR data type turns your existing SAP database into a vector store — no separate infrastructure required. Combined with the SAP embedding model through AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, you get a fully BTP-native RAG pipeline on your SAP data. This article covers the complete path: schema setup, embedding generation, chunking strategy, similarity search, and four SAP-specific RAG use cases with working code.

Series Context

This is Part 4 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–3 covered setup, Python SDK integration, and prompt pipelines. This part connects those pipelines to persistent SAP data through retrieval-augmented generation — the pattern that makes foundation model outputs grounded in your actual SAP context rather than general training data.

RAG is the architectural bridge between the prompt pipelines from Part 3 and the production SAP applications coming in Parts 5 and 6. Understanding it here makes the CAP and Joule integration articles that follow significantly easier to follow.

WHY RAG FOR SAP WORKLOADS 

Why Foundation Models Need RAG in SAP Contexts

Foundation models — even the most capable ones — have no knowledge of your SAP landscape. They do not know your custom Z-tables, your transport history, your material master conventions, your configured process variants, or the accumulated institutional knowledge in your SAP documentation. When you ask a foundation model an SAP question without context, it answers from general training data. That is not good enough for enterprise use.

RAG solves this by retrieving relevant context from your own SAP data at query time and passing it to the model alongside the question. The model's answer is grounded in your actual system — your data, your configuration, your history.

HANA Cloud makes this particularly clean for SAP practitioners because the vector store lives in the same database as your SAP data. There is no separate vector database to provision, secure, or maintain. Your embeddings sit alongside your operational data in HANA, with the same access controls, backup policies, and compliance posture you already manage.

SAP data sources → Chunk + embed → Store in HANA REAL_VECTOR → Query similarity search → Grounded model response

STEP 1: HANA VECTOR STORE SCHEMA SETUP 

Creating the REAL_VECTOR Schema in HANA Cloud

What REAL_VECTOR Is

REAL_VECTOR is a native HANA Cloud data type introduced for vector storage. It stores fixed-dimension floating-point arrays — the numerical representations (embeddings) that capture the semantic meaning of text. HANA's vector engine supports cosine similarity and L2 distance calculations directly in SQL, which means your similarity search runs inside the database without moving data out.

REAL_VECTOR is a HANA Cloud feature — it is not available in on-premise SAP HANA. Confirm your HANA Cloud instance version supports vector engine capabilities before proceeding. As of 2026, vector store functionality requires HANA Cloud QRC 1/2024 or later.

Schema Creation

Create a dedicated schema for your vector store tables. Keeping vector store tables separate from operational SAP data makes access control and maintenance significantly cleaner.

-- Create dedicated schema for vector store
CREATE SCHEMA AI_VECTOR_STORE;
 
-- General purpose document embedding table
CREATE TABLE AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (
    EMBEDDING_ID    NVARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    SOURCE_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,  -- e.g. 'KB', 'DDIC', 'TRANSPORT'
    SOURCE_ID       NVARCHAR(200)   NOT NULL,  -- original record identifier
    CHUNK_INDEX     INTEGER         NOT NULL,  -- chunk position within source doc
    CHUNK_TEXT      NCLOB           NOT NULL,  -- original text of this chunk
    EMBEDDING       REAL_VECTOR(1536),         -- embedding vector (1536 dims)
    METADATA        NCLOB,                     -- JSON metadata for filtering
    CREATED_AT      TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
 
-- Index for fast source type filtering before vector search
CREATE INDEX IDX_DOC_EMB_SOURCE ON AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID);

The EMBEDDING column dimension (1536) must match the output dimension of your embedding model exactly. The SAP embedding model used in this series outputs 1536-dimensional vectors. If you change embedding models, you must recreate the table with the correct dimension — existing embeddings cannot be reused with a different model.

STEP 2: GENERATING EMBEDDINGS VIA AI CORE 

Generating Embeddings with the SAP Embedding Model

Why the SAP Embedding Model

The SAP embedding model available through AI Core's Generative AI Hub is tuned for enterprise and business content — the kind of domain-specific language that appears in SAP documentation, functional specs, transport descriptions, and master data. For SAP-specific RAG applications, it consistently outperforms general-purpose embedding models on retrieval precision for SAP terminology and process language.

Using it through the Hub also keeps the entire pipeline within the BTP trust boundary — the same governance, audit logging, and data residency controls that apply to your generative AI calls apply to embedding generation too.

Document Chunking Strategy

Chunking — splitting source documents into segments before embedding — is one of the most consequential decisions in a RAG pipeline. Chunks that are too large dilute the embedding signal. Chunks that are too small lose context. For SAP content, the following strategy produced the best retrieval results across our four use cases:

 

Source Type

Chunk Size

Chunking Approach

SAP Knowledge Base articles

500–700 tokens per chunk

Paragraph boundaries — keep procedural steps together

HANA data dictionary descriptions

Full field/table description as single chunk

Short content — no chunking needed

Transport / change log entries

One transport record per chunk

Natural boundary — each transport is self-contained

Master data descriptions

One record per chunk with key fields as prefix

Prefix with MATNR/LIFNR for retrieval context

Embedding Generation Code

# Step 2: Generate embeddings using SAP embedding model via AI Core
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json, uuid
 
# Initialize SDK client — credentials from environment or config file
client = OpenAI()
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443, user='<user>', password='<password>', encrypt=True
)
 
def generate_embedding(text: str) -> list:
    """Generate embedding vector using SAP embedding model via AI Core."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-ada-002',
        # SAP-provided embedding model — routed through AI Core
        input=text
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding
 
def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 600, overlap: int = 50) -> list:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks by word count."""
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = ' '.join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks
 
def store_embeddings(source_type: str, source_id: str,
                     text: str, metadata: dict = None):
    """Chunk, embed, and store a document in HANA vector store."""
    chunks = chunk_text(text)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        embedding = generate_embedding(chunk)
        embedding_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
 
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            (EMBEDDING_ID, SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID, CHUNK_INDEX,
             CHUNK_TEXT, EMBEDDING, METADATA)
            VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?), ?)
        ''', (
            embedding_id, source_type, source_id, idx,
            chunk, str(embedding),
            json.dumps(metadata) if metadata else None
        ))
 
    conn.commit()
    print(f'Stored {len(chunks)} chunks for {source_type}/{source_id}')

Embedding generation has a cost per token through AI Core. For initial ingestion of large SAP document sets, run embedding generation in batches with rate limiting and store results immediately — do not regenerate embeddings on every pipeline run. Re-embed only when source documents change.

STEP 3: SIMILARITY SEARCH IN HANA SQL 

Querying the Vector Store with COSINE_SIMILARITY

How HANA Vector Search Works

HANA Cloud's vector engine computes similarity between a query embedding and all stored embeddings directly in SQL using built-in functions. COSINE_SIMILARITY measures the angle between vectors — returning values between -1 and 1, where 1 is identical. For SAP document retrieval, cosine similarity consistently outperforms L2 distance because it is scale-invariant — document length differences do not artificially inflate distance scores.

Similarity Search Function

# Step 3: Similarity search — retrieve top-k most relevant chunks
def similarity_search(query: str, source_type: str = None,
                      top_k: int = 5) -> list:
    """
    Embed query and retrieve most similar chunks from HANA vector store.
    Optionally filter by source_type for targeted retrieval.
    """
    query_embedding = generate_embedding(query)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Build query with optional source type filter
    source_filter = "AND SOURCE_TYPE = ?" if source_type else ""
    params = [str(query_embedding), top_k]
    if source_type:
        params.insert(1, source_type)
 
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT TOP ?
            SOURCE_TYPE,
            SOURCE_ID,
            CHUNK_INDEX,
            CHUNK_TEXT,
            METADATA,
            COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY_SCORE
        FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
        WHERE 1=1 {source_filter}
        ORDER BY SIMILARITY_SCORE DESC
    ''', params)
 
    results = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        results.append({
            'source_type': row[0],
            'source_id':   row[1],
            'chunk_index': row[2],
            'chunk_text':  row[3],
            'metadata':    json.loads(row[4]) if row[4] else {},
            'score':       float(row[5])
        })
    return results

Filter by SOURCE_TYPE before running vector similarity whenever you know which data domain is relevant to the query. Narrowing the search space improves both retrieval precision and query performance — especially as your vector store grows across multiple SAP data domains.

STEP 4: THE COMPLETE RAG PIPELINE 

Assembling the End-to-End RAG Pipeline

With embedding generation and similarity search in place, the RAG pipeline itself is straightforward: embed the query, retrieve relevant chunks, assemble them as context, and pass context plus query to the foundation model. The model's response is grounded in your retrieved SAP data.

# Step 4: Complete RAG pipeline function
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
def rag_query(question: str, source_type: str = None,
              top_k: int = 5, system_context: str = None) -> str:
    """
    Full RAG pipeline: retrieve relevant SAP context,
    then generate grounded response using foundation model.
    """
    # Step 1: Retrieve relevant context from HANA vector store
    retrieved = similarity_search(question, source_type, top_k)
 
    # Step 2: Assemble context from retrieved chunks
    context_blocks = []
    for r in retrieved:
        context_blocks.append(
            f"[Source: {r['source_type']} / {r['source_id']} ",
            f"| Score: {r['score']:.3f}]\n{r['chunk_text']}"
        )
    context = '\n\n---\n\n'.join(context_blocks)
 
    # Step 3: Build grounded prompt with retrieved context
    default_system = '''You are an SAP expert assistant.
    Answer questions using ONLY the context provided below.
    If the context does not contain enough information to answer,
    say so explicitly — do not use general knowledge.
    Always reference the source when citing specific information.'''
 
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=system_context or default_system),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
CONTEXT FROM SAP DATA:\n{context}
\n\nQUESTION: {question}''')
    ]
 
    # Step 4: Generate grounded response
    return llm(messages).content

The instruction 'Answer using ONLY the context provided — do not use general knowledge' is critical for SAP RAG pipelines. Without it, the model supplements retrieved context with training data, which can introduce information about standard SAP that contradicts your specific configuration or customizations.

FOUR SAP RAG USE CASES 

Use Case Examples

  ▶  Use Case 1: SAP Knowledge Base Search 

Grounded Q&A on SAP Documentation

Ingest your SAP knowledge base articles, configuration guides, and internal runbooks into the vector store. The RAG pipeline then answers practitioner questions grounded in your actual documented procedures — not generic SAP guidance from the model's training data.

# Ingest SAP knowledge base articles
kb_articles = [
    {
        'id': 'KB-2024-001',
        'title': 'HANA Delta Load: Handling Large Volume Tables',
        'content': '''When delta loading tables exceeding 500M records...
        Use hash-based partitioning with MOD(ABS(TO_BIGINT(KEY_FIELD)), N)
        to distribute load across parallel workers. Monitor memory via
        M_HEAP_MEMORY view. Control table must track per-partition watermarks.'''
    },
]
 
for article in kb_articles:
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='KB',
        source_id=article['id'],
        text=f"{article['title']}\n\n{article['content']}",
        metadata={'title': article['title']}
    )
 
# Query the knowledge base
answer = rag_query(
    question='How should I handle integer overflow in HANA delta loads?',
    source_type='KB',
    top_k=3
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 2: HANA Data Dictionary Search 

Natural Language Queries on Table and Field Metadata

Embed HANA data dictionary metadata — table descriptions, field definitions, domain values, foreign key relationships — so practitioners can find relevant tables and fields using natural language instead of needing to know exact technical names upfront.

# Ingest HANA data dictionary metadata from HANA system views
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS,
           STRING_AGG(C.COLUMN_NAME || ': ' || IFNULL(C.COMMENTS,''),  ', ')
           AS FIELD_LIST
    FROM SYS.TABLES T
    JOIN SYS.TABLE_COLUMNS C ON T.TABLE_NAME = C.TABLE_NAME
    WHERE T.SCHEMA_NAME = 'SAPHANADB'
    AND T.TABLE_NAME LIKE 'Z%'
    -- Scope to your custom Z-tables first
    GROUP BY T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS
    LIMIT 500
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    table_name, description, fields = row
    content = f'Table: {table_name}\nDescription: {description}\nFields: {fields}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='DDIC',
        source_id=table_name,
        text=content,
        metadata={'table_name': table_name}
    )
 
# Find relevant tables using natural language
answer = rag_query(
    question='Which tables store customer payment terms and credit limits?',
    source_type='DDIC',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 3: Transport and Change Log Retrieval 

Semantic Search Across SAP Change History

Transport request descriptions and change logs are high-value institutional knowledge that is almost always searched by keyword — missing semantic connections between related changes. Embedding transport history enables semantic retrieval: find transports related to a concept or problem even when the exact terms don't match.

# Ingest transport descriptions from E070 / E07T
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER,
           STRING_AGG(O.OBJECT || '/' || O.OBJ_NAME, ', ') AS OBJECTS
    FROM E070 T
    JOIN E071 O ON T.TRKORR = O.TRKORR
    WHERE T.AS4DATE >= '20250101'
    AND T.TRSTATUS = 'R'
    -- Released transports only
    GROUP BY T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    tr_id, description, date, user, objects = row
    content = f'''Transport: {tr_id}
Description: {description}
Changed by: {user} on {date}
Objects: {objects}'''
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='TRANSPORT',
        source_id=tr_id,
        text=content,
        metadata={'tr_id': tr_id, 'date': str(date), 'user': user}
    )
 
# Semantic search across transport history
answer = rag_query(
    question='What changes were made to the FI posting handler in 2025?',
    source_type='TRANSPORT',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 4: Master Data Context Retrieval for AI Classification 

Retrieval-Augmented Master Data Classification

This use case combines the master data classification pipeline from Part 3 with RAG — retrieving similar previously-classified records as few-shot examples at query time. The model classifies new records guided by real examples from your own SAP master data, not generic training examples. This produces significantly higher accuracy on company-specific classification schemes.

# Ingest already-classified master data records as reference examples
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT MATNR, MAKTX, MATKL, MTART, ZZCATEGORY
    FROM MARA JOIN MAKT ON MARA.MATNR = MAKT.MATNR
    WHERE MAKT.SPRAS = 'E'
    AND ZZCATEGORY IS NOT NULL
    -- Only records with confirmed classifications
    LIMIT 1000
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    matnr, desc, matkl, mtart, category = row
    content = f'Material: {matnr} | {desc} | Type: {mtart} | Category: {category}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='MASTER_DATA',
        source_id=matnr,
        text=content,
        metadata={'matnr': matnr, 'category': category, 'mtart': mtart}
    )
 
# Classify new material using retrieved similar records as examples
new_material = 'Hydraulic seal kit 250bar SAP-HYD-250'
 
answer = rag_query(
    question=f'Classify this new material: {new_material}',
    source_type='MASTER_DATA',
    top_k=5,
    system_context='''You are an SAP master data classifier.
    Use the similar records provided as classification examples.
    Return JSON only: {"category": "...", "confidence": "High/Medium/Low",
    "based_on": [list of similar MATNR values used as reference]}'''
)
print(answer)

Retrieval-augmented classification using your own historical master data consistently outperforms zero-shot classification because the model is guided by your specific category labels and conventions — not generic industry taxonomies. The more classified records you ingest, the more accurate the retrieval and the better the classification output.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going to Production

 

Consideration

Guidance

Embedding consistency

Never mix embedding models across records in the same table. A similarity search comparing vectors from different models produces meaningless scores. Pick one model and stick to it — if you change models, re-embed everything.

Chunk overlap

50-token overlap between chunks prevents relevant context from being split across chunk boundaries. Adjust overlap up for dense technical content like ABAP code or configuration tables.

Score thresholds

Not all retrieved chunks are relevant. Filter results below a COSINE_SIMILARITY score of 0.75 before passing to the model. Low-score chunks add noise and reduce answer quality.

Re-embedding cadence

SAP data changes. Transport descriptions, knowledge base articles, and master data classifications go stale. Build a delta re-embedding job that runs on the same schedule as your delta data loads.

Source type filtering

Always filter by SOURCE_TYPE when the query domain is known. Mixing transport history chunks with knowledge base chunks in a single retrieval pass produces lower precision than targeted retrieval from the right domain.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI with OData APIs, S/4HANA integration, and the RAG pipeline from this article embedded in a CAP service
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The vector store you build in this article carries forward into Parts 5 and 6. If you're following the series end-to-end, the RAG pipeline here is the foundation that makes the CAP application and Joule extension in the next two parts genuinely useful rather than demo-grade. Drop a comment if you hit issues with REAL_VECTOR column dimensions or similarity score thresholds — these are the two most common friction points in production deployments.

entry.title: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 26.3
entry.content:
Dear Community,

I'm happy to announce that a new release of the Mobile Development Kit (MDK) is now available for all SAP Build and Mobile Services customers. You can download it from the SAP Software Center, or from the community download page.

The SAP Mobile Services Client has been updated to version 26.3.0 on the Apple App Store and the Google Play Store.

SAP MDK enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / Visual Studio Code) and run it natively on Mobile (iOS & Android).  

Below UI enhancements are available only on iOS and Android platforms:

  • Support Styling of Key Value Item Control
  • Contact Cell Control Enhancements
  • Support In-App Camera in FormCell Attachment Control (Android Only)
  • New ActionBar Caption Size Options
  • Support Required Field Indicator for Segmented FormCell Control
  • Support Uploading Offline Store to Mobile Services
  • Onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration)

Support Styling of Key Value Item Control

MDK now supports styling Key Value item control. A new object property Styles has been introduced with following sub-properties:

  • KeyValueItem: supports only background color to style entire item.
  • KeyName: supports to style key name.
  • Value: supports to style a value property.
//excerpt of an MDK page
"KeyAndValues": [{
        "Value": "{City}",
        "_Type": "KeyValue.Type.Item",
        "_Name": "KeyValue0",
        "KeyName": "City",
        "Visible": true,
        "OnPress": "/MDK263/Actions/UpdateEntitySuccessMessage.action",
        "LinkColor": "#0000FF",
        "Styles": {
            "KeyValueItem": "KeyValueItemBackground",
            "KeyName": "KeyNameStyle",
            "Value": "KeyValueStyle"
        }
    },

//excerpt of a .less file 
.KeyValueItemBackground {
    background-color: #FFA500;
}
.KeyNameStyle {
    font-color: #ADD8E6;
    text-decoration: underline;
}
.KeyValueStyle {
    font-color: green;
}

Note:  : Value  style will take priority over LinkColor, regardless of whether OnPress is defined or not.

 

Key Value Styling on iOS and Andrid devices

 For more information, refer to the Key Value Item documentation.

Contact Cell Control Enhancements

  • Support for Initials: The ContactCell component now supports displaying user initials, providing a more personalized and recognizable visual experience when profile pictures are unavailable. This enhancement makes it easier to scan and identify contacts by replacing generic placeholder icons with meaningful initials. Three new properties have been introduced to support this feature:
    • DetailImageText: A string property that displays text in the image container when no DetailImage is present. It supports a maximum of 2–4 characters, allowing you to manually specify custom text such as initials.
    • DetailImageIsCircular: A boolean property with a default value of true that controls the shape of the image container. When set to true, the image is displayed in a circular shape; when set to false, it is displayed in a rounded rectangular shape.
    • UseHeadlineForDetailImage: A boolean property with a default value of false. When set to true and no DetailImage is provided, the control automatically extracts and displays the first two initials from the Headline property in the image container. This property takes precedence over DetailImageText.
  • Support for BadgeImage Property:  A new property, BadgeImage, is now available on ContactCell. This property allows you to attach an additional indicator icon or image to the existing DetailImage or DetailImageText for each contact. For example, it can be used to highlight and identify primary contacts in a contact list. Additionally, the existing Styles property for ContactCellItem now supports a new subproperty named BadgeImage, which enables CSS styling for the indicator icon or image defined in the BadgeImage property.
//excerpt of .page
{
    "ContactCell": {
        "_Name": "ContactCellItem6",
        "BadgeImage": "sap-icon://bell",
        "DetailImageText": "SB",
        "DetailImageIsCircular": true,
        "Headline": "Sue Bay",
        "Subheadline": "Antioch, Illinois",
        "UseHeadlineForDetailImage": false,
        "Visible": true,
        "ActivityItems": [{
                "_Name": "ContactCellItem4ActivityItems0",
                "ActivityType": "Phone"
            }
        ],
        "Styles": {
            "DetailImage": "DetailImage",
            "BadgeImage": "BadgeImage"
        }
    }
},

//excerpt of Styles.less
.BadgeImage {
    color: #068831;
    background-color: #ffae00;
}
.DetailImage {
    color: #AD00FF;
    background-color: #ADD8E6;
}

Contact Cell Control on iOS and Android devices

For more information, refer to the Contact Cell documentation.

Support In-App Camera in FormCell Attachment Control (Android Only)

We have received feedback from customers about the MDK app restarting after taking a photo using the Attachment control, especially when the app is running on Android devices with lower RAM. This may be due to memory pressure on the device, which can cause the OS to kill the MDK app running on background after launching the camera.

To address this problem, we are providing support for using an in-app camera option. This option consumes less memory, and the camera opens directly inside the MDK app instead of switching to a separate camera application.

  • A new UseInAppCamera property is available in the Application.app file under the Settings object. It determines whether the in-app camera or the device's default camera will be used for the AttachmentFormCell control’s “Take Photo” option.
    • When set to true, the in-app camera will be used instead of launching the device’s native camera application.
//excerpt of Application.app
{
    "MainPage": "/MDK263/Pages/Main.page",
    "OnLaunch": [
        "/MDK263/Rules/Service/Initialize.js"
    ],
    "Settings": {
        "UseInAppCamera": true
    },
    "_Name": "MDK263"
}

In addition, we are introducing two new APIs to manage the in-app camera settings programmatically:

  • getUseInAppCamera 
    • Retrieves the current state of the in-app camera usage flag.
    • Returns the current value determining whether the in-app camera is enabled for FormCell Attachment controls.
  • setUseInAppCamera 
    • Enables or disables the in-app camera for FormCell Attachment controls
    • Changes take effect immediately for new AttachmentFormCell instances
    • For AttachmentFormCell controls that are already rendered on the current page, the new setting will only take effect after calling the control’s reset() API. Calling the reset() API will discard any existing attachments in the control.
export default function ChangeUseInAppCamera(context) {
  let currentValue = context.getUseInAppCamera();
  context.setUseInAppCamera(!currentValue);
  alert('UseInAppCamera set to ' + !currentValue);
  let currentPageProxy = context.getPageProxy();
  if (currentPageProxy) {
    if (currentPageProxy.getName() === 'FCMain') {
      let attachmentControlProxy = context.evaluateTargetPathForAPI('#Page:FCMain/#Control:FormCellAttachment0');
      if (attachmentControlProxy) {
        attachmentControlProxy.reset();
      }
    }
  }
}

New ActionBar Caption Size Options

The ActionBar now supports flexible caption sizing through the new PreferredCaptionSize property, which replaces the existing PrefersLargeCaption property.

  • Enhanced size control: Choose from small, medium, or large caption sizes instead of toggling between only two options.
    • iOS supports small and large caption size
    • Android supports small, medium, and large caption size.

Behavior Notes:        

  • When PreferredCaptionSize is set to "large", any defined Subhead will be ignored.
  • When setting CaptionAlignment (Android only) to “center”, the PreferCaptionSize automatically set to small.  
  • On iOS, large caption mode is not supported on pages with BottomNavigation, Tabs, SideDrawer, FormCellContainer, or FlexibleColumn controls.
  • If both PreferredCaptionSize and the deprecated PrefersLargeCaption property are specified, PreferredCaptionSize takes precedence.
//excerpt of an MDK page 
"ActionBar": {
        "Items": [
            {
                "_Type": "Control.Type.ActionBarItem",
                "_Name": "ActionBarItem0",
                "Caption": "User Menu",
                "OnPress": "/MDK263/Actions/Application/UserMenuPopover.action"
            }
        ],
        "_Name": "ActionBar1",
        "_Type": "Control.Type.ActionBar",
        "Caption": "MDK 26.3",
        "PreferredCaptionSize": "Medium"
    }
}

 

Support Required Field Indicator  for Segmented FormCell Control 

In MDK 25.9 release, we introduced support for required field indicators for some FormCell controls. In this release, we are extending this support to the Segmented FormCell, indicating that the field must be filled out.
Note: This feature does not enforce users to fill out the form cell; it only provides a visual indicator that the field is required.

//Excerpt from a .page
{
    "RequiredIndicator": true,
    "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SegmentedControl",
    "_Name": "PrioritySegmented",
    "Caption": "Priority",
    "ApportionsSegmentWidthsByContent": false,
    "Segments": [
        "Low",
        "Medium",
        "High"
    ]
}

Form Cell Segmented control on iOS and Android devices

 

Support Uploading Offline Store to Mobile Services 

MDK now provides the ability for applications to upload the offline store (Both the main offline database (UDB) and the Request Queue database) to Mobile Services to assist with troubleshooting. This capability helps developers and support teams more effectively investigate and diagnose issues related to the offline store, reducing the time required to identify the root cause of problems reported by business users.
To facilitate this capability, a new MDK action has been introduced: Action.Type.OfflineOData.UploadStore.

{
    "_Type": "Action.Type.OfflineOData.UploadStore",
    "ActionResult": {
        "_Name": "UploadOfflineDB"
    },
    "Service": "/MDK263/Services/SampleServiceV4.service",
    "EncryptionKey": "MDK123", // encryption key used during the upload process
    "Note": "My Test App" // Optional note attached to the uploaded database file
}

In order to use this feature, you will need to first enable offline store upload policy in SAP Mobile Services Admin UI for a given MDK app. Developers can download the uploaded files on their local machine and use ILODATA tool to analyze them.  (see here how to use this tool). For more information, see this  documentation.

Onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration)

MDK now supports onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration). Company administrators can push onboarding settings directly to managed devices through their MDM solution.

The MDK client retrieves MDK-defined configurations from a reserved root property named MDKSettings within the Managed Configuration. Currently, this root property only accepts the ConnectionSettings object. The supported properties, their functionality, and the required structure for ConnectionSettings remain the same as in the existing implementation.

While the iOS and Android versions differ syntactically due to their file formats, they share the same structural hierarchy. Note that the actual syntax may vary depending on the requirements of the specific MDM solution being used.

  • iOS: MDM solutions for iOS commonly use an XML format for complex configurations.
  • Android: MDM solutions for Android commonly use a JSON format for complex configurations.

Become an SAP MDK Explorer!
Do you have a requirement to build a mobile solution for your enterprise? Take your first steps today by following these tutorials to get started and learn more about the SAP Mobile development kit. We’re excited to see what you build—your feedback and ideas are invaluable!

Submit Improvement Requests
You can submit your improvement request here for Mobile development kit to the SAP Customer Influence site, a central place for all product improvement requests. You can browse all improvement requests, submit improvement requests, comment, vote, receive updates, and see who has voted at the SAP Customer Influence site.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Tools 2603 Update: RAP Project Accelerator, Walkthrough, Adaptation Improvements, and More
entry.content:
With SAP Fiori tools 2603, we deliver a set of focused improvements across development transparency, onboarding, AI-supported development, and extensibility. Highlights include Project Accelerator support for RAP, a change in navigation from properties to its source, a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code, and the ability to import existing key user changes into an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project.

Project Accelerator for RAP generates full-stack SAP Fiori elements applications from text or images

One of the most important additions in this release is the extension of the Project Accelerator to the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). The Project Accelerator has been available for SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) scenarios since 2024 and supports rapid prototyping with AI, based on images and/or a requirements description. With release 2603, it can now also create ABAP artifacts and services with AI assistance, addressing a long-standing request.

This significantly broadens the reach of the Project Accelerator for enterprise SAP development. The feature is available for cloud-based RAP back ends with SAP BTP or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 or higher. For more information please refer to the SAP Help Portal page Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI. A separate blog post dedicated to this addition is currently in preparation and will be linked here once published.

The Project Accelerator lets you generate applications directly from business requirements documents, either text or images.

 

 

 

 

The Project Accelerator for RAP builds on the capabilities already available for CAP.

 

Direct navigation to source in the manifest gives developers a more transparent editing experience

Since the beginning, SAP Fiori tools has followed a clear principle: support development tasks with convenience, but do not hide the underlying technical details from experienced developers. This is why properties and settings in the tooling generally allow navigation to the corresponding source. For annotations and XML-based settings, this already opens the relevant annotation or fragment file directly. For manifest-related changes, however, SAP Fiori tools previously used a simplified virtual representation of the manifest as an intermediate editing layer.

Over time, this abstraction became more of a liability than a benefit. It introduced additional complexity, made parts of the implementation more error-prone, and did not provide enough added value compared to working directly with the actual manifest.json. With release 2603, source links for manifest properties now open the corresponding location in the real manifest and highlight the relevant section directly. This gives developers a more transparent editing experience and simplifies the path from visual tooling to source code. It also creates room for future optimizations in areas such as undo/redo handling and patch logic, which are planned for a later release.

New walkthrough for getting started makes novice developers more productive more quickly

Users who already work with SAP Fiori tools often appreciate the convenience and breadth of what the tooling offers. At the same time, the number of available capabilities can make it harder for newcomers to find a good starting point. To make onboarding easier, we have added a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code that helps users get started with SAP Fiori tools directly inside the IDE.

The walkthrough provides a practical entry point into the most relevant concepts and flows. It guides users through the initial setup and encourages hands-on exploration of the tooling. If you are new to SAP Fiori tools, or if you want a structured way to revisit the basics, try it out and complete the tasks.

The walkthrough can be started from the Help menu in VS Code, choose Open Walkthrough… and then SAP Fiori tools from the drop-down menu.

The SAP Fiori tools walkthrough explains all the capabilities and has hands-on opportunities to explore and learn.

Import key user changes into an Adaptation Project and keep your edits

With this release, when creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project, you can now import existing key user changes. This helps ensure that changes made through key user adaptation are not lost when moving to developer extensibility. The feature is available starting with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

For more background, read about key user adaptation and the transition to developer extensibility.

Additional new features in SAP Fiori tools 2603 and more planned

Release 2603 includes an update of the tools and generator to support ESLint v9. In parallel, we are internally testing a new validation feature that allows more flexible, domain-specific rules and visual integration of fix options into the Page Map and Page Editor. We plan to release this together with an initial ruleset in one of the upcoming SAP Fiori tools releases and cover it in a separate blog post.

There is also an improved support for value help services referenced by an app’s main OData V4 service. Their metadata can now be retrieved during app generation, which enables SAP Fiori tools to support scenarios such as property configuration for these value helps and mock data creation for preview. This work is part of a broader rework of test generation support that we plan to cover in a future release blog.

The MCP server for SAP Fiori can now generate an SAP Fiori elements application for an existing OData service, including services exposed from a RAP back end. It uses saved system connections from the Connection Manager extension together with a system-relative service URI. This means that the authentication flow can remain outside the AI client and the LLM, following the established SAP system sign-in flow. This is one step toward end-to-end, AI-driven, RAP-based application development in Visual Studio Code, and we will continue to expand this path in upcoming releases.

If you want to stay up to date with changes in SAP Fiori tools between release blog posts, the What’s New section in the SAP Help Portal remains the best place to track newly delivered capabilities and fixes.

The SAP Fiori development portal includes explanations and sample code for all the building blocks available for creating SAP Fiori elements applications.

entry.title: VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026: Champions Crowned at SYNC DemoJam
entry.content:
The Stage is Set
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 reached its spectacular grand finale on the main stage of VNSG SYNC 2026 – the annual VNSG and SAP Netherlands year event. In front of a packed keynote audience on 12 March, three finalist teams had exactly five minutes to demonstrate the business impact of their SAP BTP-powered innovations. The audience then cast their votes – and the results were electric.

From Challenge to Champion: The Journey
The hackathon launched in late 2025 when VNSG, SAP BTP Product Management and SAP NL Presales invited Dutch SAP customers and partners to submit real-life business challenges. Six teams were selected and, after a structured enablement phase, spent weeks co-building solutions with dedicated coaches. In February 2026, all six teams pitched to a jury panel, which selected three finalists for the grand DemoJam finale at SYNC. The three finalists stood out for their clear business relevance, technical ambition and the real-world potential to take their prototypes to production.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Champion: Promocean + SOA People — Purchase Order Control Center
Luc van Elk and Thomas Langhout from Promocean, supported by SOA People, took the audience vote and claimed the top spot. Their solution – the Purchase Order Control Center – puts SAP Business AI at the heart of procurement operations.
By connecting AI directly to SAP ECC, the team demonstrated how intelligent automation can dramatically reduce manual effort, accelerate purchase order processing, and surface exceptions before they become costly problems. A showcase of how AI on SAP BTP can deliver operational excellence in one of the most common – and pain-prone – business processes in any enterprise.

Runner-up: Ecotone + Expertum — Integration Suite Management
Arjan de Mol and Bart van de Kamp from Ecotone, partnered with Expertum Consulting, earned second place with a solution that tackles a pain many integration teams know all too well: managing and controlling large numbers of iFlows across landscapes. Their Integration Suite Management solution brings Joule-powered automation to iFlow lifecycle management – enabling operators to undeploy and deploy groups of iFlows intelligently. This turns a time-consuming, error-prone manual process into a governed, AI-assisted workflow, giving integration teams transparency, stability and control. 

Third Place: NTT DATA Business Solutions — ProTrack
Laurens Steffers and Jorrit van der Sanden from NTT DATA Business Solutions presented ProTrack – a solution delivering real-time insight for machines on the production floor. Built on SAP BTP, ProTrack addresses a critical operational need in manufacturing: knowing what is happening on your shop floor right now, not minutes later. By streaming machine data into an SAP BTP-powered dashboard, teams can react faster to exceptions, reduce downtime and optimize throughput.

 

Beyond the Podium: A Strong Field
The jury had a hard job. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen (NS), and Simac IT & BSC also built
impressive solutions that demonstrated real business value on SAP BTP. Every team that reached the jury pitch proved
that you can go from business challenge to working prototype in a short time - when you combine the right platform
with the right people. A sincere congratulations to all six participating teams !!

Behind the Scenes
Want to experience the atmosphere of the DemoJam from backstage? Watch the behind-the-scenes video - including a
live demo of how SAP Business AI is changing the way people interact with SAP, presented by Jason Enti and Ronald
Schippers: Behind the scenes at the VNSG SAP BTP DemoJam SYNC 2026

The People Who Made it Happen
A hackathon like this does not happen by itself. A huge thank you to everyone who contributed:
Presented at SYNC by: Twan van den Broek and Irene Rompa
Team coaches: Jan Penninkhof , Alex van den Hoek and Twan van den Broek
Jury: Ronald Schippersm Steven Nuyts, Steffen Schad, Jonathan Freeland
Made possible by: Mientje Paais, Olaf Winkler, Judy Bui and Niklas Siemer

What This Proves
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 is proof that innovation does not require a blank slate or a greenfield project. It requires the right platform, the right people and a real problem worth solving.
SAP BTP – combining SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Business AI and cloud-native runtimes – gives teams everything they need to move from prototype to production at speed.
The finalist solutions were not demos for the sake of demos. They addressed real procurement pain, real integration
complexity and real manufacturing visibility gaps. And they were built in weeks, not months.

What's Next?
The conversation does not end on the SYNC stage. All teams are encouraged to work with their coaches to plan production rollouts and turn hackathon prototypes into enterprise solutions.

For the broader SAP Community : Stay tuned for the next edition of the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon. Bring your own
real-life business challenge and co-build with partners and SAP experts.
And who knows - your solution could be on the SYNC main stage in 2027.

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite SAP Business AI 

@hansvp @qmrjvd @sricsi98 @BvE @mdschoenmakers @tamasszirtes @f_van_leeuwen @Petra1 @BartvdKamp @tedcastelijns @Arjan_deMol @dvvelzen @JdTeuling @AnnikaHeus @LaurensSteffers

Related reading:
When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact – VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
